textDisplay: This is exactly what I needed before tackling my shade border in October. My 'June' hostas are enormous this year and I was not sure how far back to cut. The section on large clumps at 12:00 answered my question perfectly.

Glastonbury CT gardener here too - zone 6b and we always seem to get a killing frost right when I am still deciding whether to cut back or not. This video settled it for me. Cut after the first hard frost and mulch with shredded leaves.

Great point about timing in zone 6. The key is waiting until the foliage has died back naturally after frost - that signals the plant has finished moving energy down to the crown. Cutting green foliage early can interrupt that process.

I have been growing hostas under my Norway maple for fifteen years and the slug damage every summer is demoralizing. The tip about cleaning up all the old leaf litter in fall to remove overwintering slug eggs was something I had never thought about.

Saved this one. I have about forty hostas in my shade garden and fall always feels overwhelming. The checklist approach at 18:45 is genuinely useful - I am going to print it out and walk the beds with it.

The section on reading shade types at 2:00 was a revelation. I always thought shade was shade but understanding the difference between morning sun with afternoon shade versus full dappled shade changed how I think about the north side of my house.

I watched this three times before starting my redesign. The point about not overcrowding the first year and letting hostas establish before adding more layers is something I wish I had heard five years ago.
Totally agree. The patience required in year one pays off enormously in year three and four. Shade gardens in particular reward a slow layered approach over the quick fill-it-in method.

We showed this at our spring garden club meeting. Wonderful resource. The spring ephemeral layer section at 14:30 sparked a whole conversation about trilliums and bloodroot - both native to Connecticut.

The cinnamon fern section was particularly helpful. I have a wet corner near the back of the garden where nothing has ever thrived and I think that is exactly the spot for it. The detail on soil moisture preferences at 4:00 was just what I needed.

I work with the CT Native Plant Society and I recommend this video constantly. The interrupted fern section is especially well done - it is the most underused native fern for dry shade and gardeners overlook it every time.

Thank you! The interrupted fern really does get overlooked. It handles the challenging combination of dry shade and root competition better than almost any other native fern. Excellent choice for the north and east sides of houses with maples.

Ostrich ferns have taken over a corner of my garden and I love them for it. The vase shape in spring is one of the great moments of the gardening year. My grandchildren love hunting for the fiddleheads.

The section on avoiding invasives at 15:30 is so important and often gets left out of these videos. I made the mistake of planting ajuga years ago and it has never fully left. The recommendation to source from CT Native Plant Society nursery sales is exactly right.

Good video. I would also add wild ginger, Asarum canadense, as a superb native groundcover for deep shade in Connecticut. It is slow but once established you will have it forever and it outcompetes invasives beautifully.

Wild ginger is a wonderful suggestion. We often have it at our plant sales in spring. It pairs beautifully with hostas and native ferns and its deer resistance is an underrated bonus in suburban Connecticut.

I have grown Hosta June for twelve years and this is still the best single video about it. The discussion of how the chartreuse color deepens to gold in more sun is something I observed but never fully understood until now. Mine are in morning sun and they are spectacular.

June is still the one. I have tried many others over the years but nothing touches it for that blue-green margin with the chartreuse heart. The companion planting suggestions at 14:00 are spot on - I use dark-leaved heucheras alongside mine.

I watch every Gardeners World episode and Monty's shade borders at Longmeadow are endlessly inspiring. His comment about gardening with the light you have rather than fighting it has genuinely changed how I approach my north-facing beds.
That philosophy of working with what you have is really central to how Monty approaches gardening. Shade is not a problem to be solved - it is a habitat to be planted thoughtfully. Glad it resonated.
This answered exactly the question I had about my 'June' hostas this season. The timing advice is spot on for zone 6. (October note)
I have been gardening in Connecticut for decades and this matches my experience with cinnamon fern. Thank you for the clear steps. (October note)
Saved this to my shade-garden playlist. The section on dividing large clumps was especially helpful. (October note)
My 'Blue Angel' clump has finally settled after three seasons. Videos like this gave me the confidence to leave it be. (October note)
Wonderful, practical guidance. I will be trying this with astilbe along my front walk. (October note)
The point about leaving seed heads for the birds over winter is exactly how I garden. Lovely to hear it confirmed. (October note)
As a retired librarian I appreciate a well-organized explanation, and this was beautifully structured. (October note)
Thank you — I shared this with my garden club's hostas-corner channel. Very useful for our members. (October note)
